17: ho oikos mou oikos proseuchesetai pasin tois ethnesin — my house shall be called a house of prayer for all the nations — Mark uniquely preserves the Isaianic for all nations (Isaiah 56:7) that Matthew and Luke abbreviate. The universal scope is Mark's emphasis: the temple was designed not for ethnic-Israel exclusivity but for all-peoples access to the one God. The money-changers have occupied the only court designated for Gentile prayer. The temple's failure to be the house of prayer for all nations is the specific charge. Jesus's action is the restoration of Gentile access, a theme consistent with Mark's Gentile-friendly orientation.
1: Kai hote engizousin eis Hierosolyma eis Bēthphagē kai Bēthanian pros to oros tōn elaiōn — and when they drew near to Jerusalem, to Bethphage and Bethany at the Mount of Olives — Bēthphagē (Bethphage = "house of unripe figs" in Aramaic) and Bēthania (Bethany — probably "house of affliction/poverty") are villages on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives, approximately 2 miles from Jerusalem. pros to oros tōn elaiōn (toward the Mount of Olives) — the approach from the east, the direction of the prophetic return of the divine glory (Ezekiel 43:1–5; Zechariah 14:4 where the LORD's feet stand on the Mount of Olives at the eschatological coming).
2: hypagete eis tēn kōmēn tēn katenanti hymōn, kai euthys eisporeuomenoi eis autēn heurēsete pōlon dedemenon eph' hon oudeis oupō anthrōpōn ekathisen — go into the village opposite you, and immediately on entering it you will find a colt tied on which no one has ever sat — pōlon (colt — a young animal, specifically a young donkey in the LXX; cf. Zechariah 9:9 LXX: epibebekōs epi hypozygion kai pōlon neon = riding on a beast of burden and a young colt). The unridden-upon-colt detail (eph' hon oudeis oupō anthrōpōn ekathisen) parallels requirements for animals used in sacred service (Numbers 19:2; Deuteronomy 21:3: unworked animals for holy purposes). The arrangement anticipates Zechariah 9:9's king who comes riding on a colt.
3: kai ean tis hymin eipē, Ti poieite touto? eipate, Ho kyrios autou chreian echei — and if anyone says to you: why are you doing this? say: the Lord has need of it — ho kyrios (the Lord) is the title Jesus uses for himself in this instruction — the only place in the Synoptics where Jesus uses kyrios of himself in the third person. The possessive autou chreian echei (has need of it) implies prior ownership or authority: the Lord claims the colt as his own or as available to him. euthys de auton apostellei palin hōde — and he will send it back here immediately — the temporary use with promise of return fits the pattern of commandeering for royal use.
4: kai apēlthon kai heuron pōlon dedemenon pros thyran exō epi tou amphōdou — and they went and found a colt tied at a door outside on the street — the detail fulfills the prediction of Mark 11:2 precisely: tied, at a doorway, in the street. The precision of the fulfilled instruction is characteristic of the entry's presentation as a planned prophetic act rather than an accidental encounter.
5: kai tines tōn ekei hestēkotōn elegon autois, Ti poieite lyontes ton pōlon? — and some of those standing there said to them: what are you doing, untying the colt? — the bystanders' challenge (ti poieite = what are you doing) is exactly the question Jesus anticipated in Mark 11:3. The disciples' response with the Lord's word is the test of the arrangement's validity.
6: hoi de eipan autois kathōs eipen ho Iēsous, kai aphēkan autous — and they said to them as Jesus had told them, and they let them go — the response works: aphēkan autous (they let them go) confirms that the word of the Lord was sufficient authorization. The preparation scene ends with the fulfillment of Jesus's prediction at every detail: the colt found, the challenge made, the word given, the release granted.
7: kai pherosin ton pōlon pros ton Iēsoun kai epiballousin autō ta himatia autōn, kai ekathisen ep' autō — and they brought the colt to Jesus and threw their garments on it, and he sat on it — the disciples' gesture of throwing garments (epiballousin ta himatia autōn) onto the animal is the act of acknowledging royal authority: 2 Kings 9:13 describes the commanders throwing their garments beneath Jehu when he is acclaimed king of Israel. The voluntary garment-casting on the road (Mark 11:8) will extend this gesture from a few disciples to the crowd.
8: kai polloi ta himatia autōn estōsan eis tēn hodon, alloi de stoibadas kopsantes ek tōn agrōn — and many spread their garments on the road, and others cut branches from the fields — the royal-road-preparation with garments (2 Kings 9:13) and leafy branches. John 12:13 specifies palm branches (baia tōn phoinikōn); Mark only says stoibadas (leafy branches, rushes — a general word for foliage). Palms were specifically associated with the Feast of Tabernacles and with Maccabean victory celebrations (1 Macc Mark 13:51; 2 Macc Mark 10:7), suggesting the entry activates these eschatological-victory associations.
9: kai hoi proagontes kai hoi akolouthountes ekrazon, Hōsanna! Eulogēmenos ho erchomenos en onomati kyriou — and those going before and those following were crying out: Hosanna! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord — Hōsanna is the Greek transliteration of Hebrew hōšaʿ nāʾ (Psalms 118:25 LXX: sōson dē = save now!). Originally a petition ("save, please!"), hōsanna had become a liturgical acclamation by the Second Temple period — shouted at Passover pilgrimage as an expression of joy rather than distress. Eulogēmenos ho erchomenos en onomati kyriou is Psalms 118:26 — the pilgrim-greeting for those arriving at the temple. The crowd applies it to Jesus as the specific one who comes in the Lord's name.
10: Eulogēmenē hē erchomenē basileia tou patros hēmōn Dauid — blessed is the coming kingdom of our father David — the crowd interprets the entry in Davidic-messianic terms: the basileia (kingdom) of David is the expected restoration of the Davidic monarchy. Hōsanna en tois hypsistois (hosanna in the highest — the acclamation directed upward, to the heavenly realm). Mark's crowd acclaims the kingdom rather than Jesus directly as son of David (contrast Bartimaeus in Mark 10:47–48 who directly addresses Jesus as Son of David).
11: kai eisēlthen eis Hierosolyma eis to hieron, kai periblepsamenos panta, opsias ēdē ousēs tēs hōras, exēlthen eis Bēthanian meta tōn dōdeka — and he entered Jerusalem into the temple, and looking around at everything, since the hour was already late, he went out to Bethany with the Twelve — periblepsamenos panta (looking around at everything — the characteristic Markan scanning gaze; cf. Mark 3:5,34; Mark 5:32; Mark 9:8; Mark 10:23). The survey is strategic: Jesus comes in, looks at everything, makes no immediate dramatic action, and leaves. The anti-climactic departure sets up the temple-action of Mark 11:15–19 as a deliberate return rather than a spontaneous outburst. Mark's structure — entry, withdrawal, return with action — underscores the intentionality of the temple-cleansing.
12: Kai tē epaurion exelthontōn autōn apo Bēthanias epeinase — and on the following day as they were leaving Bethany, he was hungry — epeinase (was hungry — aorist; genuine physical hunger). The fig tree cursing that follows is framed as arising from genuine hunger, making the symbolic interpretation non-obvious from the story's surface level. Mark preserves the detail without explaining it; the interpretation is layered rather than explicit.
13: kai idōn sykēn apo makrothen echousan phylla ēlthen ei ara ti heurēsei en autē, kai elthōn ep' autēn ouden heuren ei mē phylla, ho gar kairos ouk ēn sykōn — and seeing a fig tree from a distance having leaves, he came to see if he would find anything on it, and coming to it he found nothing except leaves, for it was not the season for figs — ho gar kairos ouk ēn sykōn (for it was not the season for figs) is Mark's aside that creates the interpretive difficulty: Jesus curses a fig tree for bearing no fruit when it is not fruit-season. In the Mediterranean fig cycle, a tree leafing in spring before Passover should have borne the small early figs (bikkurim, early figs) even before the main crop. A tree in full leaf with no early figs is a tree that shows signs of life but has no fruit — precisely the symbol for the temple establishment that will follow in Mark 11:15–19.
14: kai apokritheis eipen autē, Mēketi eis ton aiōna ek sou mēdeis karpon phagoitō — and answering he said to it: may no one eat fruit from you ever again — apokritheis (answering — as if to an implied question; the fig tree's full leaves implied a claim to fruitfulness that is now answered). The curse is absolute: mēketi eis ton aiōna (no longer for ever). The optative phagoitō (let eat — the prayer-optative of wish). Mark sandwiches the temple-cleansing between the cursing (Mark 11:14) and the withering (Mark 11:20–21), a structural technique (the Markan intercalation) that interprets each through the other: the fig tree and the temple are mutually interpretive symbols.
15: Kai erchontai eis Hierosolyma, kai eiselthōn eis to hieron ērxato ekballein tous pōlountas kai tous agorazontas en tō hierō — and they came to Jerusalem, and entering the temple he began to drive out those selling and buying in the temple — ekballein (to drive out, to cast out — the same verb as exorcism). The action is against both sellers (tous pōlountas) and buyers (tous agorazontas) — the whole commercial enterprise in the temple courts. The money-changers' tables and dove-sellers' benches are overturned. The violence is directed at the commercial apparatus, not at persons.
16: kai ouk ēphien hina tis denenken skeuos dia tou hierou — and he would not allow anyone to carry merchandise/a vessel through the temple courts — the prohibition of using the temple as a thoroughfare (diapherein skeuos = carry a vessel/implement through) indicates that the outer courts (the Court of the Gentiles) had become a commercial shortcut as well as a marketplace. This detail, unique to Mark, intensifies the desecration: not merely commerce in the sacred space but using it as a path for ordinary commercial traffic.
18: Kai ēkousan hoi archiereis kai hoi grammateis kai ezētoun pōs auton apolēsōsin, ephobounto gar auton — and the chief priests and scribes heard and were seeking how they might destroy him, for they feared him — ezētoun pōs auton apolēsōsin (were seeking how to destroy him — imperfect, ongoing seeking: from this point the execution-plot is in motion). ephobounto gar auton (for they feared him — the reason: not guilt about the temple-arrangements but fear of his popular authority). hoti pas ho ochlos exeplēsseto epi tē didachē autou — for all the crowd was astonished at his teaching — the crowd's astonishment (ekplēssō) creates the political constraint: direct arrest risks popular uprising.
19: Kai hotan opse egeneto, exeporeuonto exō tēs poleōs — and whenever evening came, they went out of the city — the daily pattern during the Jerusalem week: Jesus and the disciples retire to Bethany each evening (Mark 11:11; Mark 11:19; implied Mark 14:3). The withdrawal to Bethany (Mary, Martha, Lazarus's village) is the overnight base from which each day's Jerusalem-ministry is conducted.
20: Kai paraporeouomenoi prōi eidon tēn sykēn exērammenen ek rhizōn — and passing by in the morning they saw the fig tree withered from the roots — exērammenen (withered, dried up — perfect passive participle of xērainō; the same verb as the withered hand of Mark 3:1, the withered-rigid boy of Mark 9:18, and the dried-up spring of Mark 5:29). ek rhizōn (from the roots) emphasizes the totality of the withering: not just the leaves but the root-system. The tree is dead, not merely leafless. The perfect tense indicates a completed state: the withering has occurred and the result stands at the time of discovery.
21: kai anamnēstheis ho Petros legei autō, Rhabbi, ide hē sykē hēn katērasō exērrantai — and Peter remembering says to him: Rabbi, look! the fig tree which you cursed has withered — anamnēstheis (having remembered — the same verb as the disciples' remembering at 14:72 after the denial). Peter connects the withered tree to the curse of the previous day. katērasō (you cursed — aorist; the curse was definitive and its effect is visible). The observation opens the teaching on faith and prayer that follows (Mark 11:22–25).
22: kai apokritheis ho Iēsous legei autois, Echete pistin theou — and answering Jesus says to them: have faith of God — pistin theou (faith of God — the genitive is ambiguous: "faith in God" [objective genitive, standard translation] or "God-quality faith" [genitive of quality/source]; the context [mountain-moving, prayer-receiving] supports the former but the genitive of quality is also grammatically possible). The command shifts from the withered fig tree to the principle it illustrates: the power to curse and the power to move mountains both derive from faith-toward-God in prayer.
23: amēn legō hymin hoti hos an eipē tō orei toutō, Arthēti kai blēthēti eis tēn thalassan, kai mē diakrithē en tē kardia autou alla pisteusē hoti ho lalei ginetai, estai autō — truly I tell you that whoever says to this mountain: be lifted up and thrown into the sea, and does not doubt in his heart but believes that what he says happens, it will be for him — tō orei toutō (to this mountain — "this mountain" in the Jerusalem context likely refers to the Temple Mount or the Mount of Olives; the metaphor of mountain-moving is a Semitic idiom for doing something extremely difficult or impossible). mē diakrithē en tē kardia autou (does not doubt in his heart — diakrinō = to judge between, to waver between two assessments; the heart divided between trust and doubt). The condition is singleness of faith, not quantity.
24: dia touto legō hymin, panta hosa proseuchomenoi aiteisthe, pisteuete hoti elabete, kai estai hymin — therefore I tell you, all things whatever you ask in prayer, believe that you have received them, and they will be for you — pisteuete hoti elabete (believe that you received — aorist, past: the belief treats the answer as already given). The aorist elabete (you received) rather than the present or future is the distinctive grammatical note: the prayer of faith treats the petition as already answered in God's purpose, not merely hoped for in the future. This is not a formula for commanding God but the description of how faith engages with divine promise.
25: kai hotan stēkete proseuchomenoi, aphiete ei ti echete kata tinos, hina kai ho patēr hymōn ho en tois ouranois aphē hymin ta paraptōmata hymōn — and when you stand praying, forgive if you have anything against anyone, in order that your Father who is in heaven may also forgive you your trespasses — the standing posture for prayer (stēkete proseuchomenoi) is the standard Jewish prayer posture (Luke 18:11; Acts 7:55). The connection of forgiveness-given and forgiveness-received mirrors the Lord's Prayer (Matthew 6:12,14–15); Mark places it here in connection with efficacious prayer. paraptōmata (trespasses, false steps — from para- + piptō = to fall beside the path, to deviate; the word for moral failure as a deviation from the right course).
26: Verse 26 — "but if you do not forgive, neither will your Father who is in heaven forgive your trespasses" — is absent from the oldest manuscripts (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus, and others) and is regarded by UBS5/NA28 as a secondary insertion from Matthew 6:15. The verse is a negative formulation of Mark 11:25's principle; the original Markan text states the positive without the corresponding negative. The interlinear tradition preserves it as a textual reality; most modern critical editions omit it or bracket it.
27: Kai erchontai palin eis Hierosolyma. kai en tō hierō peripatountos autou erchontai pros auton hoi archiereis kai hoi grammateis kai hoi presbyteroi — and they came again to Jerusalem, and while he was walking in the temple the chief priests and scribes and elders came to him — the full Sanhedrin delegation: archiereis (chief priests — the temple hierarchy), grammateis (scribes — legal scholars), presbyteroi (elders — lay aristocratic leadership). These are the three groups named in the passion predictions (Mark 8:31; Mark 10:33) who will condemn Jesus to death; their approach here is the confrontation that begins the final Jerusalem controversy cycle.
28: kai elegon autō, En poia exousia tauta poieis? ē tis soi edōken tēn exousian tautēn hina tauta poiēs? — and they said to him: by what authority are you doing these things? and who gave you this authority to do these things? — poia exousia (what kind of authority — the question about the nature and source of authority). The question is legitimate: prophetic, priestly, and royal authority were the recognized categories; Jesus has acted with the authority of all three without possessing any of their official credentials. The double question (en poia exousia + tis soi edōken) presses both the nature and the source of the authority.
29: Ho de Iēsous eipen autois, Eperwēsō hymas hena logon, kai apokrithēte moi, kai erō hymin en poia exousia tauta poiō — and Jesus said to them: I will ask you one question; answer me, and I will tell you by what authority I do these things — the counter-question technique was common in rabbinic controversy: to answer a question with a question, or to make the questioner's answer to a prior question determine the answer to the original question. Jesus does not evade the question but sets a precondition for answering it: they must first answer his question.
30: To baptisma to Iōannou ex ouranou ēn ē ex anthrōpōn? apokrithēte moi — John's baptism — was it from heaven or from humans? Answer me — the question is a dilemma: John's baptism was widely regarded by the people as prophetic and authoritative; to deny it a heavenly origin would anger the crowds; to affirm it as heavenly places them in a bind about why they did not accept John's testimony to Jesus. ex ouranou (from heaven — the Jewish circumlocution for God; "heaven" used as a substitute for the divine name). The two options are exclusive and exhaustive: either divine or human origin, no middle ground.
31: kai dielogizonto pros heautous legontes, Ean eipōmen, Ex ouranou, erei, Dia ti [oun] ouk episteusate autō? — and they deliberated among themselves saying: if we say "from heaven" he will say: why then did you not believe him? — the deliberation (dielogizonto — the internal reasoning-together verb) is exposed to the reader: the leaders debate which answer will cause them less damage. If they say "from heaven," they must explain their rejection of John. The conditional is exact: John's preaching included pointing to Jesus (Mark 1:7–8); to affirm John's heavenly authority is to implicitly affirm John's testimony to Jesus.
32: alla eipōmen, Ex anthrōpōn — ephobounto ton ochlon, hapantes gar eichon ton Iōannēn hoti ontōs prophētēs ēn — but if we say "from humans" — they feared the crowd, for all held that John was really a prophet — the second option is equally dangerous: ephobounto ton ochlon (they feared the crowd). hapantes gar eichon ton Iōannēn hoti ontōs prophētēs ēn (for all held John to be truly a prophet) — the universal popular conviction makes "from humans" politically impossible. The leaders are trapped between theological honesty (which would commit them to Jesus) and political safety (which requires them to deny John's divine authority).
33: kai apokrithentes tō Iēsou legousin, Ouk oidamen. Kai ho Iēsous legei autois, Oude egō legō hymin en poia exousia tauta poiō — and answering Jesus they say: we do not know. And Jesus says to them: neither will I tell you by what authority I do these things — Ouk oidamen (we do not know) is the calculated evasion: they know their answer but will not say it. Jesus's response is exact: the precondition he set (answer my question) is unmet; the promised answer (his authority-source) is therefore withheld. The refusal is not punitive but logical: a debate about authority with people who will not honestly address the one prior authority-case is pointless. The question of Jesus's authority will be answered definitively at Mark 14:62 — under oath before the Sanhedrin.
